name: Samaria
history: Samaria, located in the central highlands of the West Bank, was the capital of the ancient Kingdom of Israel before its conquest by the Assyrian Empire in 722 BCE. The region has been inhabited continuously through various periods including Persian, Hellenistic, Roman, Byzantine, and Islamic rule. During the Ottoman era, Samaria became part of the broader Levantine territories, and in modern times it has been at the center of Israeli-Palestinian territorial disputes following the 1967 Six-Day War. Today it remains a contested region with significant historical and religious importance to both Israelis and Palestinians.
culturalNotes: Samaria is characterized by its rugged mountainous terrain and agricultural heritage, with communities deeply rooted in both Palestinian and Israeli traditions. The region is known for its Palestinian villages that maintain strong cultural practices, including traditional crafts, olive cultivation, and close-knit family structures. For Israeli settlers in the area, the region holds deep biblical and historical significance, reflected in their connection to the ancient Kingdom of Israel and commitment to the land.
bestTimeToVisit: April to May or September to October for comfortable exploration of historical sites.
languageNotes: Arabic is the primary language spoken by Palestinians in Samaria, with some variation in local dialects, while Hebrew is spoken by Israeli residents and settlers. Visitors will find that basic Arabic phrases like 'Marhaba' (hello) and 'Shukran' (thank you) are appreciated by locals, and Hebrew speakers are common in Israeli communities throughout the region.

places.name: Nablus
places.description: The largest city in Samaria, Nablus is an ancient commercial hub famous for its Old City with narrow souks, soap factories, and culinary traditions. It's a vital center of Palestinian culture and offers authentic markets, traditional sweets, and historical Ottoman architecture.

activities.detail: Wander through the maze of narrow covered markets selling spices, textiles, and traditional crafts in the heart of Nablus's historic center.
Visit one of the few remaining traditional olive oil soap factories to see the centuries-old production methods and purchase authentic Palestinian soap.
Tour this sacred site revered by Muslims, Christians, and Jews, located on the outskirts of the city in a historically significant location.
Admire the Ottoman-era architecture and intricate stone work of this prominent mosque in the Old City.
Ascend to this sacred mountain overlooking Nablus to visit the Samaritan temple site and enjoy panoramic views of the Samarian region.

food.detail: A fragrant upside-down rice dish layered with eggplant, tomatoes, and chicken or lamb, then flipped onto a platter and served with yogurt.
Nablus's signature dessert made of shredded pastry soaked in sweet syrup and cheese, baked until golden, and eaten warm.
Thin bread topped with sumac-seasoned chicken, caramelized onions, and pine nuts, a beloved Palestinian specialty.
A variant of kunafa featuring creamy kashta (clotted cream) between layers of crispy pastry, characteristic of Nablus street food culture.
Fresh, crispy falafel made from chickpeas and served with creamy hummus at the numerous souk vendors throughout the Old City.

culture.detail: Visitors should wear modest clothing covering shoulders and knees, particularly when visiting mosques and religious sites.
Learning basic Arabic phrases like 'As-salamu alaikum' (peace be upon you) is appreciated and opens doors to genuine local interactions.
In the souks, negotiating prices with vendors is a normal part of the shopping experience and often appreciated as part of market culture.
Always ask before photographing people, particularly in the souks and residential areas, as some residents prefer privacy.

places.name: Mount Gerizim
places.description: This sacred mountain overlooks Nablus and is a holy site for Samaritans, with a museum and archaeological remains dating back millennia. Visitors can hike to the summit for panoramic views of the West Bank and enjoy insights into one of the world's oldest religious communities.

activities.detail: Trek to the 881-meter peak for panoramic views of Nablus, the surrounding Palestinian territories, and Jordan Valley.
Explore artifacts and exhibits documenting the history and religious practices of the Samaritan community, one of the world's oldest monotheistic faiths.
Walk among archaeological remains of the Second Temple period, including foundations and stone structures dating back over 2,000 years.
If visiting during Passover, witness the ceremonial lamb sacrifice and feast performed by the Samaritan community on the mountain, a tradition maintained for centuries.
Capture sweeping landscape photography from multiple vantage points along the summit ridge overlooking Nablus and the West Bank valleys.
View pottery shards, coins, and religious artifacts excavated from the site, displayed with contextual information about Samaritan and earlier settlements.

food.detail: Dense, unleavened flatbread traditionally baked by Samaritan families, often served with olive oil and za'atar, reflecting millennia of culinary heritage.
Shredded pastry dessert layered with white cheese and topped with pistachios or walnuts, a specialty of the Nablus region at the mountain's base.
Slow-cooked lamb seasoned with traditional spices, reflecting the community's historical connection to pastoral life and religious rituals.
Creamy chickpea dip sourced from Nablus vendors below the mountain, often flavored with tahini and lemon in the Palestinian style.
Sample locally-pressed olive oil from Samaritan and Palestinian groves surrounding Mount Gerizim, often served with fresh bread.

culture.detail: Mount Gerizim remains a living sacred site for Samaritans; dress modestly, avoid interrupting prayers or ceremonies, and ask permission before photographing religious artifacts or gatherings.
The Samaritan community numbers fewer than 900 people today; showing genuine interest in their ancient traditions and current practices is appreciated and enriches your visit.
Verify current access routes and security conditions before visiting, as the site's proximity to Nablus and political sensitivities can affect visiting hours and safety.
Hiring a knowledgeable local guide—ideally connected to the Samaritan community or Nablus tourism—provides context on 3,000 years of history and authentic cultural insights.

places.name: Ramallah
places.description: A vibrant Palestinian city south of Samaria known for its contemporary culture, cafes, galleries, and relatively cosmopolitan atmosphere. It serves as a cultural and political center with good restaurants, shops, and the iconic Arafat Mausoleum.

activities.detail: Visit the white-domed memorial complex honoring the late Palestinian leader, located in the heart of Ramallah with both historical and political significance.
Explore the striking architectural building featuring Palestinian history, art, and culture across multiple galleries and outdoor installations.
Catch live performances, theater productions, and cultural events in this important venue for Palestinian arts and entertainment.
Walk through the central streets to discover independent art galleries, photography exhibitions, and contemporary Palestinian artwork displayed throughout the city.
Visit the campus area north of Ramallah known for its intellectual atmosphere, student life, and occasional cultural events open to visitors.
Spend time at traditional Palestinian coffee houses where locals gather to drink strong coffee, smoke shisha, and engage in conversation.

food.detail: A Palestinian specialty of roasted chicken served on taboon bread topped with caramelized onions, sumac, and pine nuts, quintessentially Palestinian.
Creamy chickpea paste topped with spiced ground beef, pine nuts, and olive oil, best eaten fresh at local restaurants for breakfast or lunch.
Crispy deep-fried chickpea fritters stuffed into pita bread with tahini sauce, fresh vegetables, and pickled turnips, a common street food.
Lamb cooked in a yogurt-based sauce with herbs, served over rice or bread, representing traditional Palestinian hospitality cuisine.
A warm dessert made of shredded pastry, white cheese, and honey syrup, best enjoyed fresh from bakeries in the early morning.
Sample locally pressed extra-virgin olive oil from the surrounding region, often sold in markets and used generously in Palestinian cooking.

culture.detail: Palestinians are traditionally warm and hospitable; expect offers of strong coffee or tea, and accepting at least one cup is appreciated as a sign of respect.
While Ramallah is more cosmopolitan than surrounding areas, wearing conservative clothing—especially in older neighborhoods—shows respect for local customs.

Be aware that Ramallah is the de facto political center of the Palestinian territories; avoid insensitive comments about the Israeli-Palestinian conflict and respect the significance of local monuments.
Many businesses close during Friday prayers; plan activities accordingly and note that social life often centers on family gatherings in the afternoon and evening.

places.name: Sebastiya
places.description: An archaeological site perched on a hilltop near Nablus, Sebastiya contains ruins from the Roman, Byzantine, and Crusader periods, including the tomb of John the Baptist. The panoramic views and well-preserved remains make it one of the region's most significant historical destinations.

activities.detail: Walk among the impressive row of standing columns from the 1st-century Roman city, which once formed the main street of Sebaste.

activities.text: Visit the Tomb of John the Baptist
activities.detail: Descend into the underground chamber believed to house the relics of John the Baptist, a site sacred to Christians, Muslims, and Samaritans.

activities.text: Survey Panoramic Views from the Summit
activities.detail: Stand at the hilltop's highest point to view sweeping vistas across the Samaria region, the Jordan Valley, and beyond.
Examine the foundations and mosaics of the 5th-century Byzantine basilica dedicated to John the Baptist, built atop earlier structures.
Climb the partially preserved medieval tower from the 12th-century Crusader occupation, offering views and insight into the site's medieval history.
Trace the footsteps of Roman and Byzantine residents along original paved roads that connect different quarters of the ancient city.
Study the defensive fortifications built during the Hellenistic period that remain partially visible on the site's lower slopes.

food.detail: This signature Palestinian dish of chicken baked atop taboon bread with caramelized onions and sumac is commonly served in nearby Nablus restaurants and local eateries.
A beloved Palestinian dessert of shredded phyllo with melted cheese and sweet syrup, best sampled fresh from local bakeries in the Nablus area near Sebastiya.
Fresh chickpea falafel and creamy hummus topped with olive oil and pine nuts are staple street foods available throughout the region.
This traditional lamb stew cooked in yogurt sauce is a celebratory dish served in local restaurants and guesthouses on special occasions.
Local producers near Sebastiya offer premium olive oil and the region's signature za'atar spice blend, ideal for purchase as authentic regional products.

culture.detail: The tomb of John the Baptist is revered by multiple faiths; remove shoes before entering underground chambers and maintain quiet reverence in religious areas.
A Palestinian guide from Sebastiya village can provide crucial context on the site's religious significance, recent history, and practical navigation of the archaeological remains.
The site has minimal facilities and no artificial lighting, so plan your visit for mid-morning through late afternoon to safely explore ruins and enjoy photography.
Locals appreciate visitors who greet them with 'As-salamu alaikum' (peace be upon you) and simple courtesies in Arabic, which enhances cultural exchange.

places.name: Jacob's Well
places.description: Located in Nablus, this biblical well is one of the oldest continuously used wells in the world and is mentioned in the Gospel of John. It sits beneath a Greek Orthodox church and offers both spiritual and historical significance for pilgrims and visitors.

activities.detail: Walk down steep stone steps into the underground chamber where the well itself lies, allowing you to touch the ancient water source that has been used for over 2,000 years.

activities.text: Pray at the Greek Orthodox Church
activities.detail: Visit the Church of St. Photina built directly above the well, featuring Byzantine architecture and religious icons that commemorate the Samaritan woman's encounter with Jesus.
Reflect on the Gospel of John Chapter 4 account at the exact location where Jesus met the Samaritan woman, one of Christianity's most significant theological encounters.
Observe the visible geological and structural evidence showing continuous use from the Patriarchal period through Roman, Byzantine, and modern times.
Tour the nearby camp's heritage museum to understand the broader Palestinian experience and context of the surrounding region.
Hike to the nearby Samaritan holy site offering panoramic views of Nablus valley and the well's location within its landscape.

food.detail: A Palestinian dish of lamb cooked in fermented yogurt sauce and served over rice, traditionally eaten during family gatherings and celebrations in Nablus.
An inverted rice and vegetable or meat casserole native to Nablus that is flipped onto a plate just before serving for dramatic presentation.
A sweet pastry with shredded phyllo dough, cheese, and pistachio from Nablus, considered the finest version of this dessert throughout the Levant.
Local pita bread topped with za'atar herb mixture and olive oil, widely available from street vendors and bakeries around Nablus's Old City.
Experience thick, cardamom-infused coffee served in small cups as a gesture of hospitality, an essential social ritual when meeting locals.

culture.detail: Wear conservative clothing covering shoulders and knees when visiting the church and well, as it is an active sacred religious site for Orthodox Christians.
Be prepared to pass through Israeli security checkpoints when traveling between Israeli territory and Nablus, and allow extra travel time accordingly.
Ask permission before photographing inside the church and well chamber, as some areas may be restricted during prayer times or special services.
Visit during afternoon hours when worship services are less frequent, or inquire locally about the best times to avoid disrupting active religious observances.

places.name: Jenin
places.description: A northern Palestinian city in Samaria known for its agricultural produce, particularly olives and citrus fruits, and its artistic community. The city has a lively marketplace and serves as an important cultural and economic hub in the northern West Bank.

activities.detail: Browse the traditional marketplace filled with spice vendors, textile stalls, and local crafts reflecting Palestinian heritage and commerce.
Explore exhibits documenting the city's history, traditional Palestinian life, and cultural artifacts in this restored heritage building.
Visit the surrounding agricultural terraces and olive groves during harvest season to see traditional Palestinian farming methods firsthand.
Attend local theatrical performances, art exhibitions, and cultural events showcasing Palestinian contemporary and traditional arts.
Day trip to nearby Arraba to experience traditional Palestinian village life, stone architecture, and local hospitality in the countryside.

food.detail: A traditional Palestinian rice dish layered with vegetables and meat, then inverted onto a platter, signature to the northern West Bank region.
Chicken roasted with sumac, onions, and pine nuts served on taboon bread, a beloved Palestinian specialty particularly popular in Jenin.
Sample freshly pressed olive oil and wild herb blend at markets, key ingredients reflecting Jenin's agricultural heritage.
Enjoy freshly fried falafel and creamy hummus made from local chickpeas at family-run eateries throughout the city center.
Drink freshly squeezed orange and lemon juice from Jenin's renowned citrus orchards, available at juice stands in the souk.

culture.detail: Many shops close during the five daily Islamic prayer times, so plan activities and shopping accordingly.
Wear conservative clothing covering shoulders and knees out of respect for local Islamic customs and cultural norms.
Learning basic Arabic phrases like 'As-salamu alaikum' (peace be upon you) is appreciated and opens doors to genuine local interactions.
Palestinian hosts typically offer coffee or tea as a sign of welcome; accepting even a small cup is considered respectful and customary.

places.name: Qalqilya
places.description: Situated near the Israeli border, Qalqilya is known for its agricultural heritage and greenhouses producing vegetables and flowers. The city offers glimpses into Palestinian rural life and agricultural traditions in the West Bank.

activities.detail: Browse the bustling central market where local farmers sell fresh produce, herbs, and flowers directly from Qalqilya's renowned greenhouses.
Visit working agricultural greenhouses to see how local farmers cultivate tomatoes, cucumbers, flowers, and herbs using traditional Palestinian farming methods.
Explore exhibits on Palestinian history, archaeology, and traditional village life in this modest but meaningful cultural institution.
Take day trips to surrounding farming communities like Azzun and Kafr Thulth to experience authentic Palestinian village rhythms and agricultural practices.
Visit the weekly Friday market where farmers converge to sell seasonal produce, flowers, and local goods in a lively community gathering.

food.detail: A signature Palestinian dish of roasted chicken with sumac, caramelized onions, and pine nuts served on taboon bread, reflecting Palestinian culinary heritage.
Sample salads and dishes made with ultra-fresh tomatoes, cucumbers, and leafy greens picked directly from local greenhouses that morning.
Flatbread topped with wild thyme za'atar, olive oil, and sesame seeds, commonly sold by street vendors and bakeries throughout the city.
A Palestinian rice and vegetable dish cooked in one pot with chicken or lamb, then inverted onto a platter, often served at family meals and special occasions.
Enjoy traditional herbal infusions made from locally grown chamomile, mint, and other medicinal flowers and herbs from Qalqilya's gardens.

culture.detail: Show genuine interest in farming practices and acknowledge the significance of greenhouse work to the community's identity and livelihood.
Spring and early summer offer the best experience to see the greenhouses in full production and participate in seasonal market activity.
Wear conservative clothing that covers shoulders and knees, particularly when visiting family-run farms or entering community spaces.
A few words of Arabic, particularly about agriculture and greetings, will be greatly appreciated by local farmers and vendors.

places.name: Tulkarem
places.description: A northwestern Palestinian city famous for its agricultural production and industrial zones, particularly textile and food processing. It offers authentic Palestinian markets and a genuine glimpse into daily life in the northern West Bank.

activities.detail: Browse the bustling traditional Palestinian bazaar selling fresh produce, spices, textiles, and handicrafts that reflect the city's agricultural heritage.
Visit one of the region's oldest refugee camps to understand Palestinian history and community life, with guided tours available through local organizations.
Explore exhibits documenting Tulkarem's farming traditions, olive oil production, and rural Palestinian life over generations.
Visit active textile factories and workshops where traditional Palestinian fabrics and embroidered goods are produced and sold directly.
Tour this neighboring agricultural village known for olive groves and greenhouses, offering a quieter rural perspective on West Bank life.

food.detail: A signature Palestinian rice dish layered with eggplant, tomatoes, and chicken or lamb, then flipped upside-down onto a plate, commonly prepared in family kitchens throughout Tulkarem.
Roasted green wheat grains cooked with chicken or vegetables, a staple grain dish reflecting the region's cereal crop heritage.
Sample locally pressed olive oil drizzled with za'atar (thyme-sesame blend) and warm pita bread, especially during the autumn harvest season.
A sweet pastry dessert with shredded phyllo, cheese, and pistachios soaked in sugar syrup, available at local bakeries and cafés throughout the city.
Flatbread topped with sautéed onions, sumac, and chicken, a beloved Palestinian dish frequently served at family gatherings and restaurants.

culture.detail: Palestinians in Tulkarem deeply value hospitality and will often offer strong coffee or tea; accepting graciously shows respect and helps build genuine connections.
Visitors should wear conservative clothing that covers shoulders and knees, particularly when visiting markets, mosques, or rural areas, as a sign of respect for local customs.
Learning basic Arabic greetings like 'As-salamu alaikum' (hello) and 'Shukran' (thank you) will be warmly received and enhance your interactions with locals.
Much of daily social activity revolves around family and communal spaces; visiting public markets and cafés in afternoons and evenings offers the best glimpses of authentic community life.

places.name: Sabastia Village
places.description: A Palestinian village surrounding the archaeological ruins of Sebastiya, offering traditional stone houses and a rural atmosphere combined with historical exploration. Visitors can experience village life while exploring Roman-era remains and enjoying hillside vistas.

activities.detail: Walk among the partially reconstructed columns from the 2nd-century Roman city of Sebastiya, which once honored Emperor Augustus.
View artifacts and exhibits documenting the site's Hellenistic, Roman, Byzantine, and Islamic periods in a modest village museum.

activities.text: Hike the hillside paths around the village
activities.detail: Follow traditional stone-lined paths through olive groves and terraced farmland offering panoramic views of the West Bank landscape.

activities.text: Visit the Greek Orthodox Church of St. John the Baptist
activities.detail: Explore this 19th-century church built over older Byzantine foundations, believed to mark the burial site of John the Baptist's head.
Watch local artisans craft traditional Palestinian ceramics and earthenware using hand-spinning wheels and local clay.
Experience the architecture of centuries-old village homes with distinctive arched doorways and thick stone walls built into the hillside.

food.detail: Palestinian flatbread topped with sumac-spiced roasted chicken, onions, and pine nuts, a signature dish of the Nablus region.
Warm village bread brushed with olive oil and covered with the herbal spice blend of thyme, sumac, and sesame seeds.

food.text: Fresh olive oil from local harvest
food.detail: Sample cold-pressed extra virgin olive oil produced by village families from surrounding groves, especially during autumn pressing season.
Grape leaves rolled with rice, herbs, and sometimes meat, then steamed—a staple prepared fresh by village families.
Creamy strained yogurt served with olive oil, fresh mint, and wild herbs, eaten with warm pita bread.

culture.detail: Wear long pants or skirts and covered shoulders to respect conservative village customs and religious sensitivities.
If invited to a home, expect to be offered strong coffee or tea and accept at least one cup as a sign of respect and building goodwill.
Plan your visit between sunrise and sunset, as village infrastructure and facilities are most accessible during daylight.
Always request consent before taking photographs of residents, particularly women and children, as privacy is deeply valued.

places.name: Wadi Qana
places.description: A scenic nature reserve and valley known for its spring waters, hiking trails, and natural biodiversity in the heart of Samaria. The wadi offers a refreshing escape with opportunities for walking, picnicking, and experiencing the region's natural landscape.

activities.detail: Wade and swim in the natural freshwater pools fed by Wadi Qana's perennial springs, offering cool relief year-round.
Follow the well-worn path through the wadi canyon, passing limestone cliffs and lush vegetation along the 4-5 km route.
Set up a meal on the grassy banks beside the flowing water, surrounded by tamarisk and fig trees.
Spot Syrian woodpeckers, Palestine sunbirds, and other Mediterranean species in the riparian habitat.
Capture the canyon walls glowing amber and gold as evening light reflects off the water and stone formations.
Identify endemic and medicinal plants including wild mint, oleander, and date palms that thrive in the microclimate.

food.detail: Local vendors near the wadi prepare small freshwater fish caught from the springs, seasoned simply with olive oil and lemon.
A Levantine mixed salad with wild greens foraged from the wadi region, tomatoes, cucumbers, and toasted pita chips dressed in sumac vinaigrette.
Creamy chickpea dip from nearby Palestinian settlements, served with warm flatbread and a drizzle of local olive oil.
Refreshing herbal tea brewed with fresh wild mint harvested from the wadi banks, traditionally served in small glass cups.
Flatbread brushed with olive oil and topped with the spice blend of thyme, sumac, and sesame, baked fresh in wood-fired ovens nearby.

culture.detail: Wadi Qana's status as a Palestinian nature reserve means entry may be limited or require local permission; check current access conditions before visiting.
Although bathing in the pools is common, wear modest swimwear and cover exposed skin when not in the water out of respect for local norms.
The wadi is a protected ecological area; visitors are expected to leave no trace and carry out all waste to preserve the natural environment.
Spring (March–May) offers lush vegetation and reliable water flow, while summer heat can be intense; avoid visiting during heavy winter rains when water levels rise dangerously.

places.name: Nablus Soap Factories
places.description: Historic soap-making workshops in Nablus's Old City that have produced olive oil soap for centuries using traditional methods passed down through generations. These fragrant factories are open to visitors and offer insights into this iconic Palestinian craft and commerce.

activities.detail: Observe craftsmen mixing olive oil, water, and soda ash in large copper vats, then pouring the mixture into wooden molds using techniques unchanged for centuries.
Walk through the workshop spaces where soap blocks are cut, stacked, and cured in traditional drying rooms filled with the aroma of olive and laurel oils.
Speak with factory owners or guides who explain the cold-process soap-making technique and the historical significance of Nablus soap in regional trade.
Buy directly from the factory shop, selecting from varieties including pure olive oil soap, laurel-scented blocks, and gift sets wrapped in traditional packaging.
Capture the visual richness of stacked soap blocks, steam rising from vats, and the weathered stone architecture of the Old City workshop buildings.

food.detail: This warm Palestinian dessert of shredded pastry, melted cheese, and syrup is a signature sweet sold in nearby Old City bakeries and cafés.
Freshly made chickpea purée with tahini and lemon is available at local restaurants throughout Nablus's Old City, often topped with olive oil from the region.
Deep-fried chickpea or fava bean balls seasoned with cumin and coriander are sold at street stands near the soap factories and served in pita bread.
This traditional Palestinian dish of roasted chicken with sumac, onions, and pine nuts served on bread is a staple lunch in family-run restaurants nearby.

culture.detail: Factories may close or have limited access during the five daily Islamic prayer times, particularly Friday midday prayers when the Old City observes community worship.
Wearing covered shoulders, knees, and avoiding tight clothing shows respect in this conservative Palestinian area and helps visitors blend respectfully with the community.
Expect to negotiate prices when buying soap directly from factories or shops; friendly bargaining is part of the traditional marketplace culture and often expected.
Learning basic phrases like 'As-salamu alaikum' (peace be upon you) and 'Shukran' (thank you) is appreciated and opens warmer interactions with soap makers and locals.

restaurants.name: Hamaayan
restaurants.description: Contemporary Israeli restaurant known for seasonal menus and locally sourced ingredients in a modern setting.

restaurants.name: Kibbutz Shiloh Restaurant
restaurants.description: Farm-to-table dining at a working kibbutz with panoramic views of the Samarian hills and organic produce.

restaurants.name: Satya
restaurants.description: Well-established Indian restaurant offering authentic curries and tandoori specialties in a casual atmosphere.

restaurants.name: HaZone Cafe
restaurants.description: Casual cafe serving quality coffee, pastries, and sandwiches popular with locals.

restaurants.name: Restaurant Wadi
restaurants.description: Authentic Palestinian cuisine in a family-run establishment known for hummus and traditional mezze.

restaurants.name: Mizpe Shiloh Lookout Cafe
restaurants.description: Simple hilltop cafe with stunning views, serving coffee, light meals, and fresh juices.

restaurants.name: Tapas Ariel
restaurants.description: Spanish-style tapas bar with excellent wines and a social dining atmosphere.

restaurants.name: Abu Shukri
restaurants.description: Legendary hummus restaurant in the Old City souk, operating since 1967 with family recipes.

restaurants.name: BBQ House Ariel
restaurants.description: High-quality meat restaurant with professional grilling and attentive service.

restaurants.name: Falafel Al-Reef
restaurants.description: No-frills falafel stand serving crispy falafel in fresh pita with tahini sauce.

restaurants.name: Meron Restaurant
restaurants.description: Upscale dining featuring Mediterranean cuisine with emphasis on fresh vegetables and olive oil.
restaurants.mustOrder: Sea bream with lemon and herbs

restaurants.name: Zaatar w Zeit
restaurants.description: Fast-casual chain specializing in zaatar-based dishes and fresh Middle Eastern sandwiches.

restaurants.name: Hagiviya
restaurants.description: Popular local grill house known for grilled meats, chicken, and traditional side dishes.

restaurants.name: Al-Manara Restaurant
restaurants.description: Traditional Palestinian restaurant with a rooftop setting and classic dishes like mansaf and musakhan.
